agency: NYC Department for the Aging (DFTA / NYC Aging)
method: Outside-in crawl (browser UA, robots-respecting — nyc.gov/robots.txt only disallows /html/misc/). Fingerprinted www.nyc.gov/site/dfta (Akamai + nginx + NYC.gov 'Livesite' platform v22 + Dynatrace) and the resident-facing 'Find Help' / Aging Connect intake at nyc.gov/site/dfta/services/find-help.page (Aging Connect information-and-referral contact center, 212-AGING-NYC / 212-244-6469 — phone/web, no API). Verified the NYC Open Data agency label 'Department for the Aging (NYC Aging)' via the Socrata Discovery API and pulled all 11 assets with column schemas.
headline_findings: DFTA (NYC Aging) is a connective, network-funding agency: it rarely delivers services itself, it funds ~1,000 community providers — so its reference data is a directory of providers, sites, senior-center operations, activities, and contract service units.

The provider/program reference data is well OPEN: 11 NYC Open Data datasets covering all contracted providers (38 columns), publicly open sites, older adult center (senior center) Local Law 140 operations, activities, budgeted/reported service units, expenditures, and aggregate participation.

But the resident SERVICE layer — connecting an older adult to case management, home-delivered meals, benefits, caregiver support, elder-abuse help, or senior center enrollment — has no machine-readable surface at all: intake happens only by calling Aging Connect (212-AGING-NYC), calling 311, or walking into a center.
Client/participant data is published only in aggregate (Number of Participants, unduplicated clients); DFTA never exposes an individual older adult record to Open Data.
Net-new write surface: make a service referral (ServiceReferral) — connect an older adult to services — the core resident transaction, with no API today. See tech-stack.md and apis-observed.md.

apis_observed.note: 11 DFTA datasets; SODA /resource/<id>.json endpoints. The one real, open DFTA API — provider/program reference data only.

apis_observed.public: phone / walk-in / web form, no API
apis_observed.note: The intake and referral layer — case management, home-delivered meals, benefits, caregiver, elder abuse, center enrollment. No JSON/OpenAPI; a contact center, not a system with a machine-readable surface.
content only; Akamai edge, nginx, Dynatrace RUM. No content API.
alternate front doors New Yorkers use to reach aging services and screen for benefits.

fruit.name: Contracted Service Providers
opendata_match: List of Providers with Sites Open to the Public u7wp-np5k (21c)
fruit.notes: Core unit of the DFTA network. Keyed on DFTA ID; provider types include Older Adult Center, Case Management, Home-Delivered Meal, Caregiver, Homecare, NORC, Elder Abuse, Geriatric Mental Health, Legal, Transportation, New York Connects. Sponsor, hours, address, full geography spine.

fruit.name: Older Adult Centers (senior centers)
opendata_match: Senior Center Local Law 140 Provider Data ygfr-ij6t (49c)
Senior Center Local Law 140 Client Data hm83-bdp7 (12c)
fruit.notes: Senior-center operating profile under Local Law 140 — utilization, meals served, staffing, budget/expenditures, days open. An OlderAdultCenter is a ServiceProvider of type 'Older Adult Center', enriched with center operations.

fruit.name: Older Adult Center Activities
fruit.notes: Scheduled classes, meals, recreation, and health-promotion events at older adult centers; virtual/in-person, recurring, accessibility, topics, geography. The browse surface an older adult uses to find something to do near them.

fruit.name: Contract Service Units & Budgets
fruit.notes: Budgeted-vs-reported service units and amounts per provider/service/contract year — the contract-performance line behind the network.

fruit.name: Program Participation (aggregate)
opendata_match: Number of Participants in NYC Aging funded Programs 2td3-mfek (18c)
fruit.notes: Unduplicated client counts per program/site/fiscal year — AGGREGATE ONLY. No individual older adult record is ever published (privacy by design).

fruit.name: Find help / get connected to services
fruit.notes: The general 'find help' front door — routes to Aging Connect (212-AGING-NYC). Phone/walk-in only; no API.

fruit.name: Apply for home-delivered meals
fruit.notes: Request home-delivered meals for a homebound older adult — assessed and arranged via Aging Connect / a case-management agency. No API. An alternative net-new write surface.

fruit.name: Request case management
fruit.notes: Connect an older adult to a Case Management Agency for benefits, entitlements, and care planning — Aging Connect intake only; no API.

fruit.name: Report suspected elder abuse
fruit.notes: Urgent elder-abuse / unsafe-at-home referral — routed to Aging Connect / Adult Protective Services. Phone only; the urgent path of the net-new write surface.

fruit.name: Make a service referral
fruit.notes: The single most common resident interaction — connect an older adult (self, family, or caseworker) to DFTA services and track the referral. No API and no Open Data twin; lives only in the phone-based Aging Connect intake or a 311 call. The net-new WRITE surface for this domain.

fruit.name: Local Law 140 OAC Metrics FY2022
fruit.notes: LL140 Older Adult Center operating metrics FY2022 (utilization, meals, staffing, budget).

fruit.name: Local Law 140 Senior Center Metrics FY2021
Local Law 140 Metrics FY2023
fruit.notes: LL140 older adult/senior center metrics FY2023.

fruit.name: Local Law 140 Metrics FY2024
fruit.notes: LL140 older adult/senior center metrics FY2024.

fruit.name: Local Law 140 Metrics FY2025
fruit.notes: LL140 older adult/senior center metrics FY2025 (latest).

meta.platform: NYC.gov shared 'Livesite' platform (Akamai + nginx + Dynatrace) — no separate vendor system; the service layer is a phone-based Aging Connect information-and-referral contact center.
meta.netnew: ServiceReferral — connect an older adult to DFTA services (case management, meals, benefits, caregiver, elder abuse, center enrollment) with an urgent path to Aging Connect / Adult Protective Services.
meta.tagline: The provider network is open data; the act of connecting an older adult to a service is a phone call.
meta.headline: DFTA publishes its ~1,000-provider network generously on Open Data, but the resident's core transaction — getting connected to a service — has no API, only the Aging Connect phone line.

scorecard_notes.platform: Shared NYC.gov Livesite chassis; no DFTA-owned application platform, service layer is a phone contact center.
scorecard_notes.siteApi: No site or content API; nothing machine-readable on nyc.gov/site/dfta.
scorecard_notes.openData: 11 open Socrata datasets covering the provider network well, but no service-layer data.
scorecard_notes.coreData: Core provider/center/activity/service-unit entities are modeled and open; participation is aggregate-only.
scorecard_notes.writeApi: No write surface at all — every referral/intake is a phone call to Aging Connect.
scorecard_notes.agent: No agent-native surface; no MCP, no structured intake an agent could call.
scorecard_notes.ownership: Reference data is DFTA-labeled on Open Data, but the transaction layer is an unowned contact-center process.
